recovery_prompt: Check if the command is installed. Try: 'which <command>' or install it with the appropriate package manager. Verify the command name spelling and check your PATH.
Verify the file path exists. Use 'ls' to check the directory. Check for typos in the path, and ensure you're in the correct working directory with 'pwd'.
Check file permissions with 'ls -la'. Try adding execute permission with 'chmod +x' or run with appropriate privileges. Verify ownership with 'stat'.
The command ran but returned a failure exit code. Check stderr output for details. Run the command with increased verbosity (--verbose, -v, --debug) to get more information.
Exit code 127 means the command was not found. Verify the command name, check if it's installed, and ensure it's in your PATH. Try 'which <command>' to locate it.
Exit code 126 means the command was found but is not executable. Add execute permission with 'chmod +x <file>', or check if the file has the correct shebang line.

A segmentation fault indicates a memory access violation. This is usually a bug in the program. Try: (1) run with a different input, (2) check for known bugs, (3) try a different version, (4) run under gdb/lldb for debugging.
The process ran out of memory. Try: (1) reduce input data size, (2) close other processes, (3) increase swap space, (4) use a machine with more RAM, (5) process data in smaller batches.

Broken pipe means the receiving process closed the connection. This often happens with piping to 'head' or similar. Try: (1) ensure the receiving process is running, (2) buffer the output, (3) redirect to a file first.
A process is already using that port. Try: (1) 'lsof -i :<port>' or 'netstat -tlnp' to find the process, (2) kill the existing process, (3) use a different port, (4) set SO_REUSEADDR in your code.
Disk is full. Try: (1) 'df -h' to check space, (2) 'du -sh * | sort -rh | head' to find large files, (3) clean up with 'rm' or 'docker system prune', (4) check /tmp and cache directories.
The operation requires elevated privileges. Try: (1) run with sudo if appropriate, (2) check file permissions, (3) verify you own the resource, (4) check if the filesystem is mounted read-only.
The filesystem is mounted read-only. Try: (1) remount as read-write 'mount -o remount,rw <device>', (2) check if it's a disk error forcing read-only mode, (3) use a different filesystem for writes.
The resource is in use by another process. Try: (1) 'fuser' or 'lsof' to find the process using it, (2) kill the process, (3) unmount the resource first, (4) wait and retry.

pattern: cannot execute binary file|exec format error
recovery_prompt: The binary file format doesn't match your platform. Try: (1) check if it's the right architecture (x86 vs ARM), (2) verify the file is not corrupted, (3) check the shebang line for scripts, (4) try 'file <binary>' to inspect it.
The file lacks execute permission. Add it with 'chmod +x <file>'. Also verify the shebang line (#!/bin/bash, #!/usr/bin/env python3, etc.) is correct.

pattern: bash[:\s].*syntax error|unexpected token|unexpected end of file
recovery_prompt: A bash syntax error occurred. Check for: (1) missing quotes or brackets, (2) unescaped special characters, (3) missing 'fi', 'done', 'esac' closures, (4) incorrect variable expansion syntax.
Node.js/npm is not installed or not in PATH. Install with: 'curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash - && sudo apt install -y nodejs' or use nvm.
Python is not installed or the command alias is wrong. Try: (1) use 'python3' instead of 'python', (2) install Python via pyenv or system package manager, (3) create a virtual environment.
Docker is not installed. Install with the official script: 'curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh'. Then add your user to the docker group: 'sudo usermod -aG docker $USER'.
Git is not installed. Install it: Ubuntu/Debian: 'sudo apt install git', macOS: 'brew install git', or check your PATH.

npm install failed. Try: (1) delete node_modules and package-lock.json, then reinstall, (2) clear npm cache: 'npm cache clean --force', (3) check for network issues, (4) try with '--legacy-peer-deps'.
pip install failed. Try: (1) upgrade pip: 'pip install --upgrade pip', (2) install build dependencies, (3) use a virtual environment, (4) try '--no-cache-dir', (5) check Python version compatibility.
Make is not installed. Install build essentials: Ubuntu: 'sudo apt install build-essential', macOS: 'xcode-select --install'.

Git merge conflict. Try: (1) 'git status' to see conflicted files, (2) manually resolve conflicts in each file, (3) 'git add' resolved files, (4) 'git commit' to complete the merge. Or abort with 'git merge --abort'.

pattern: sudo.*password is required|a password is required
recovery_prompt: The command requires sudo and a password. Either: (1) provide the password, (2) configure passwordless sudo in visudo, (3) find an alternative command that doesn't require sudo.
The process was killed, likely due to OOM (out of memory). Try: (1) reduce memory usage, (2) process data in smaller chunks, (3) increase available memory, (4) check 'dmesg' for OOM-killer messages.

Connection refused means no service is listening on that port. Try: (1) verify the service is running: 'ss -tlnp | grep <port>', (2) start the service, (3) check firewall rules, (4) verify the host and port are correct.
Connection timed out. Try: (1) check network connectivity, (2) increase timeout, (3) try a different endpoint, (4) check if a firewall is blocking, (5) verify DNS resolution.
SSL certificate verification failed. Try: (1) install the CA certificate, (2) update the cert bundle, (3) for development only: set REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE or --insecure flag (never in production).

pattern: argument list too long|command line too long
recovery_prompt: The command line exceeds the OS limit. Try: (1) use 'xargs' to batch arguments, (2) use 'find -exec', (3) pipe file lists, (4) use a script file instead of inline commands.
Too many open file descriptors. Try: (1) increase ulimit: 'ulimit -n 65536', (2) close unnecessary files, (3) check for file descriptor leaks, (4) use connection pooling.
Resource temporarily unavailable. Try: (1) retry after a short delay, (2) increase system limits, (3) check for resource exhaustion, (4) use non-blocking I/O with backoff.
The file or directory doesn't exist at that path. Verify the path with 'ls', check for typos, ensure you're in the right directory, and handle spaces in filenames properly.

Bash ambiguous redirect error. Check for: (1) unquoted variable expansions, (2) glob patterns that match multiple files, (3) missing file descriptor, (4) use quotes around variable references in redirects.
The file is being modified while another process reads it. Try: (1) write to a temp file first, then move atomically with 'mv', (2) wait for the writing process to finish, (3) use lock files.
A core dump indicates a crash. Try: (1) analyze the core file with gdb: 'gdb <binary> <core>', (2) check if the program has a known bug, (3) try a different version, (4) enable more logging.

error_message: shell session or terminal not found
pattern: not a tty|no controlling tty|inherits not a tty
recovery_prompt: No TTY available for interactive commands. Try: (1) use non-interactive flags (-q, --no-input, --batch), (2) pipe input via stdin, (3) use 'script' command, (4) set TERM=dumb.

Bad interpreter usually means the shebang line points to a non-existent interpreter. Check the shebang line (#!/...) and ensure the interpreter path exists. Use '#!/usr/bin/env <lang>' for portability.
npm network error. Try: (1) check internet connectivity, (2) clear npm cache: 'npm cache clean --force', (3) switch to a different registry, (4) set HTTP_PROXY, (5) increase timeout in .npmrc.
Node.js ENOENT error. The file or directory does not exist. Try: (1) verify the path, (2) create parent directories first, (3) check working directory, (4) use path.resolve() for absolute paths.
Node.js EACCES permission error. Try: (1) change file/dir ownership, (2) modify permissions with chmod, (3) avoid running as root, (4) use npm config set prefix ~/.npm-global for global installs.
The installed package's scripts directory is not in PATH. Add it: 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' or install with '--user' flag and add the path to your shell profile.

pattern: docker.*permission denied|Got permission denied while trying to connect
recovery_prompt: Docker permission denied. Add your user to the docker group: 'sudo usermod -aG docker $USER', then log out and back in. Or use 'sudo docker' temporarily.
Bash syntax error. Check for: (1) mismatched quotes (' vs "), (2) missing semicolons or newlines, (3) unescaped special characters, (4) copy-paste issues with smart quotes. Use shellcheck to lint.
GPG key error. Try: (1) create a key: 'gpg --gen-key', (2) or import existing key, (3) set GPG_TTY: 'export GPG_TTY=$(tty)', (4) use passphrase-less key for CI.

error_message: unable to lock the administration directory
pattern: unable to lock|dpkglock|another process is using
recovery_prompt: Another package manager process is running. Try: (1) wait for it to finish, (2) 'sudo rm /var/lib/dpkg/lock-frontend /var/lib/dpkg/lock', (3) 'sudo dpkg --configure -a', (4) never force this on production systems.
Shell variable error. The variable may be readonly or unset. Try: (1) use a different variable name, (2) check if it's exported correctly, (3) use ${var:-default} for safe defaults.
curl error 6: Couldn't resolve host. DNS resolution failed. Try: (1) verify the URL is correct, (2) check DNS: 'nslookup <host>', (3) try a different DNS server, (4) check /etc/hosts.
curl error 7: Failed to connect. The server rejected the connection. Try: (1) verify the host and port, (2) check if the service is running, (3) check firewall rules, (4) try 'telnet <host> <port>'.
curl error 35: SSL connect error. Try: (1) update CA certificates, (2) check TLS version compatibility, (3) try --tlsv1.2 flag, (4) check for cipher mismatch.

npm dependency resolution conflict. Try: (1) 'npm install --legacy-peer-deps', (2) update package.json to match peer dependency versions, (3) 'rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json && npm install', (4) check for incompatible package versions.
Cannot remove a non-empty directory. Try: (1) 'rm -rf <dir>' to force remove, (2) check contents first with 'ls -la <dir>', (3) remove files inside first then remove the directory.
wget is not installed. Install it: Ubuntu/Debian: 'sudo apt install wget', macOS: 'brew install wget', or use 'curl -O' as an alternative.
Port is already in use. Find the process: 'lsof -i :<port>' or 'ss -tlnp | grep <port>'. Kill it: 'kill <PID>'. Or use a different port.
Disk quota exceeded. Try: (1) check your quota with 'quota -s', (2) delete unnecessary files, (3) compress large files, (4) request a quota increase from your admin.

pattern: already running|pid file exists|is already running
recovery_prompt: The process is already running. Try: (1) check with 'ps aux | grep <process>', (2) remove stale PID file if process is not actually running, (3) kill the existing process, (4) use --force flag if supported.
